title: Empty Space That Pushes the Universe Apart
summary: Cosmic voids are huge empty areas in space that look like nothing is there, but they are not truly empty. They are filled with something called spacetime vacuum, which has energy. This energy is called dark energy, and it makes the universe expand faster. In most places, like Earth or galaxies, matter is so strong that dark energy doesn't do much. But in voids, where there is very little matter, dark energy becomes powerful and pushes space apart. This means voids are where the universe grows bigger the most. Scientists study voids to understand dark energy better and learn how the universe changes over time.

questions.question: Why doesn't dark energy affect Earth much?
What do scientists study voids to understand?

background_read: Cosmic voids are like giant bubbles in space where there is almost no stars, planets, or other matter. They are important because they help scientists learn about dark energy, a mysterious force that makes the whole universe grow bigger. On Earth, we don't feel dark energy because everything around us is packed with matter, but in voids, it's the main thing pushing space apart. Understanding this helps us know how the universe started and where it's going.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists and physicists study cosmic voids.
WHAT: Cosmic voids are empty areas where dark energy pushes the universe apart.
WHERE: In space, especially in cosmic voids far from galaxies.
WHY: To understand how dark energy works and how the universe expands.

perspectives.description: Scientists see voids as key places to study dark energy and universe expansion.
